hypothesis: If models trained with equivariance-inducing regularization (such as equigrad) achieve representational alignment comparable to architecturally equivariant models despite lacking built-in symmetry constraints, then systematically fine-tuning a non-equivariant general-purpose large language model on molecular property prediction tasks using equivariance-inducing loss terms will produce latent representations that align more strongly with state-of-the-art equivariant MLIPs than those of architecturally equivariant models trained on narrower chemical domains, demonstrating that learned physical inductive biases can substitute for architectural ones as a route to universal chemical representations.
